description: Sample idea briefs the visitor can load into The Gauntlet with one click. Each entry is a fully-structured brief grounded in homeland security, CISA Critical Infrastructure Sectors, and national security subject-matter expertise. Visitor picks a sample, brief is written into sessionStorage tg_visitor_brief, welcome modal pre-fills, and they walk the EP corridor + Chamber as if they had written it themselves.

samples.title: Sentinel
samples.tagline: Field-deployable synthetic opioid detection for mass-casualty triage
samples.summary: A ruggedized, handheld detection platform that identifies fentanyl, nitazenes, and novel synthetic opioid analogs at the scene within 90 seconds, transmitting confirmed compound data to an incident command dashboard for real-time antidote pre-positioning.
samples.brief_text:
SENTINEL — Field-Deployable Synthetic Opioid Detection

CONCEPT
A ruggedized, field-portable detection system enabling first responders, HAZMAT teams, and law enforcement to positively identify synthetic opioids — including fentanyl analogs, carfentanil, and emerging nitazene compounds — at the scene of a suspected mass-casualty event within 90 seconds of sample collection. Detection results transmit in real time to an incident command dashboard that tracks exposure zones and pre-positions antidote supplies.

PROBLEM
Synthetic opioids have fundamentally changed the CBRN threat landscape. Illicitly manufactured fentanyl (IMF) and its analogs are now detectable in counterfeit pharmaceuticals, adulterated street drugs, and increasingly in potential weaponization scenarios assessed by federal partners. First responders arriving on scene cannot distinguish compound identity by visual inspection alone. Standard colorimetric fentanyl strips produce binary yes/no results and fail on novel analogs. Naloxone dosing for carfentanil or nitazene exposure requires compound-specific guidance that field teams currently lack. Victims die during the identification gap.

SOLUTION
Sentinel combines surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) with a cloud-connected incident management layer:
- Compound Identification Module: SERS cartridge system identifies synthetic opioids and analogs against a continually updated reference library maintained in partnership with DEA forensic labs. Positive ID confirmed in under 90 seconds.
- Incident Command Dashboard: each Sentinel device transmits compound identity, GPS coordinates, and timestamps to a shared operational picture accessible by incident command, hospital system liaisons, and regional HAZMAT coordinators.
- Antidote Pre-Positioning Protocol: dashboard calculates naloxone volume requirements based on confirmed compound identity and estimated exposure numbers; transmits pre-positioning recommendations to regional pharmacy caches and hospital emergency departments before the first transport arrives.
- Chain-of-Custody Log: cryptographically signed detection records support law enforcement evidence requirements for federal prosecution.

TARGET USERS
- Municipal and county HAZMAT teams
- Law enforcement tactical and patrol units in high-IMF-prevalence regions
- Emergency Medical Services incident command
- FBI WMD Coordinators and Joint Terrorism Task Force field teams
- Military CBRN units (CWMD applications)

COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE
Existing field tools (Bruker ALPHA II, 908 Devices MX908) provide laboratory-grade mass spec or FTIR in portable form but require operator training above the patrol officer level, carry high per-unit cost ($30K–$80K), and lack the incident management layer. Sentinel targets the detection-to-decision pipeline as the primary product, not just the sensor.

KEY RISKS TO ADDRESS
- Novel analog evasion: reference library currency depends on DEA/DHS forensic data-sharing agreements that must be negotiated pre-launch.
- Legal chain-of-custody: evidence admissibility of field SERS data varies by jurisdiction; legal review required state by state.
- Naloxone supply chain: pre-positioning recommendations are only as good as real-time pharmacy inventory data; integration contracts with distributors are required.
- First-responder training burden: any device requiring more than a 4-hour certification is unlikely to achieve patrol-level deployment.

CRITICAL INFRASTRUCTURE SECTOR
Healthcare and Public Health / CBRN-WMD. Ties directly to DHS BioWatch and FEMA Mass Casualty Incident frameworks. Complements existing National Opioid Crisis response infrastructure.

NEXT STEPS
Secure a SBIR/STTR Phase I with NIJ or BARDA to fund the SERS cartridge validation study. Identify a HAZMAT unit willing to serve as a pilot partner for field testing. Draft the DEA forensic data-sharing memorandum of understanding. Engage regional hospital networks for the antidote pre-positioning protocol pilot.

samples.title: PRISM
samples.tagline: Pre-operational indicator documentation across fusion center classification levels
samples.summary: A structured documentation platform that standardizes behavioral pre-operational warning signs across fusion centers operating at different classification tiers, enabling cross-jurisdictional case handoffs without losing behavioral context or violating compartmentalization.
samples.brief_text:
PRISM — Pre-Operational Risk Indicator Synthesis Module

CONCEPT
A structured information system that standardizes the collection, documentation, and cross-agency sharing of behavioral pre-operational indicators in terrorism and domestic violent extremism investigations. PRISM applies a Structured Professional Judgment (SPJ) framework to threat assessment documentation, enabling case officers at different classification levels — federal, state fusion center, local law enforcement — to hand off behavioral context without violating compartmentalization or starting over from zero.

PROBLEM
The 9/11 Commission's core finding — that the government possessed the dots but could not connect them — remains structurally unremediated at the behavioral documentation layer. Fusion centers today operate with inconsistent documentation standards for pre-operational behavioral warning signs. A case assessed by an FBI Joint Terrorism Task Force field team at SECRET level cannot cleanly hand off behavioral context to a state fusion center operating at CUI or unclassified. Investigators at the receiving agency reconstruct from scratch. Behavioral patterns that a trained analyst would recognize as a coherent pre-attack signature fragment across a handoff into disconnected incident reports.

SOLUTION
PRISM is a classification-aware behavioral documentation platform:
- SPJ Checklist Engine: based on validated threat assessment frameworks (WAVR-21, HCR-20, B-SAFER) adapted for terrorism and DVE contexts. Structured behavioral observation fields prevent the free-text degradation that makes cross-case pattern recognition impossible.
- Classification Tiering: case data is entered at the appropriate classification level. PRISM generates a Behavioral Context Summary (BCS) — a deidentified, unclassified distillation of the behavioral narrative — that can be shared laterally without classification violation.
- Case Handoff Protocol: receiving agencies access the BCS through a credentialed portal. The BCS preserves the behavioral arc (grievance → radicalization indicators → mobilization signals) without exposing source or method details.
- Pattern Correlation: PRISM's backend flags cases sharing behavioral indicator combinations across fusion centers, enabling regional pattern recognition that individual case officers cannot see from within their jurisdiction.
- Chain of Custody: all behavioral assessments are timestamped and signed, supporting admissibility in federal prosecution.

TARGET USERS
- FBI Joint Terrorism Task Force field teams
- State and regional fusion center threat assessment units
- DHS Office of Intelligence and Analysis
- Campus and venue threat assessment teams requiring federal case linkage
- InfraGard member organizations flagging behavioral concerns related to critical infrastructure sectors

COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE
Existing reporting platforms (FBI eGuardian, DHS Suspicious Activity Reporting) are intake tools, not behavioral assessment or case handoff tools. They capture tip data, not structured behavioral analysis. No existing system provides SPJ-based behavioral documentation with classification-aware cross-agency sharing.

KEY RISKS
- Interoperability with existing classified networks: PRISM requires Authority to Operate (ATO) at the appropriate classification levels. This is a multi-year process and the primary technical risk.
- Civil liberties: behavioral documentation systems carry inherent risk of mission creep into surveillance of protected First Amendment activity. Clear scope controls and independent audit mechanisms are required.
- Adoption: fusion centers operate with significant autonomy. Federal mandate or funding incentive likely required for uniform adoption.

CRITICAL INFRASTRUCTURE SECTOR / FOLDER
Terrorism & DVE. Cross-sector applicability: behavioral pre-operational indicators do not respect sector boundaries; the same system applies to infrastructure threats, mass casualty planning, and targeted violence.

NEXT STEPS
Convene a working group with representatives from DHS I&A, FBI JTTF, and two state fusion centers to map current documentation inconsistencies. Commission a legal review of the BCS sharing protocol under EO 12333 and applicable Privacy Act provisions. Draft a pilot MOU for a two-fusion-center handoff test case.

samples.title: AuditTrace
samples.tagline: Automated foreign-influence detection for federally funded STEM grant applications
samples.summary: A platform that ingests federal grant applications, cross-references PI affiliations against foreign talent recruitment program databases and BIS/OFAC entity lists, and generates a Research Security Risk Score to supplement institutional grants office review.
samples.brief_text:
AUDITTRACE — Foreign Research Influence Detection for Federal Grants

CONCEPT
A platform that automates the research security screening layer for federally funded STEM grant applications. AuditTrace ingests grant application packages, cross-references principal investigator (PI) and co-investigator affiliations against curated databases of foreign talent recruitment programs, BIS Entity List participants, and OFAC-designated entities, then generates a Research Security Risk Score and supporting evidence package for the institutional grants officer and program officer at the funding agency.

PROBLEM
Foreign research influence — primarily but not exclusively through People's Republic of China-linked talent recruitment programs (Thousand Talents, Chang Jiang, and derivatives) — has resulted in technology transfer from federally funded university research to foreign military and commercial programs. The National Security Presidential Memoranda on research security and NSPM-33 direct federal agencies to implement disclosure verification. But compliance is uneven: PIs self-certify foreign affiliations on paper; grants offices lack the staffing and database access to verify; program officers at funding agencies have no systematic screening tool. Undisclosed affiliations reach funding decisions.

SOLUTION
AuditTrace is a research security screening platform:
- Application Ingestion: accepts grant application documents (PDF, XML export from Grants.gov) and extracts PI, co-I, and key personnel identifiers.
- Affiliation Cross-Reference: checks extracted identifiers against:
  - Known foreign talent recruitment program participant lists (compiled from public congressional testimony, DOJ prosecutions, published academic research)
  - BIS Entity List and Unverified List
  - OFAC SDN List
  - Co-authorship graph analysis flagging undisclosed foreign co-investigators
- Risk Score Generation: produces a tiered Research Security Risk Score (Low / Elevated / High) with a supporting evidence package citing specific matches and co-authorship patterns.
- Grants Officer Dashboard: grants officers review flagged applications with the evidence package before submission to the funding agency. High-risk flags trigger a disclosure verification workflow with the PI before the application advances.
- Program Officer API: funding agency program officers access AuditTrace scores as a supplemental input in their review workflow, not as an automatic disqualifier.

TARGET USERS
- R1 university grants and contracts offices
- Federal funding agency program officers (NSF, NIH, DOE, DARPA, ONR)
- University research compliance and export control officers
- Congressional oversight staff monitoring NSPM-33 implementation

COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE
Existing tools include commercial entity screening platforms (World-Check, Kharon) designed for financial compliance, not research security. Academic co-authorship analytics tools (Dimensions, Scival) provide network data but no entity list cross-reference or disclosure verification workflow. No integrated research security screening platform exists at the grants office layer.

KEY RISKS
- Database currency: foreign talent recruitment program lists are not maintained by a single authoritative government source. Data curation requires ongoing human analyst input.
- False positive rate: co-authorship with foreign nationals is normal scientific practice; the risk scoring model must distinguish legitimate collaboration from undisclosed covered relationships. High false positive rate destroys adoption.
- Legal liability: a grants officer acting on an AuditTrace flag must not create a discriminatory adverse action based on national origin alone. Legal review of the scoring model's use-policy is required.
- Classification: some of the most actionable foreign talent program data is classified. Unclassified AuditTrace will have gaps that only a classified variant can address.

CRITICAL INFRASTRUCTURE SECTOR / FOLDER
AI Governance & Research Security. Cross-sector applicability to Defense Industrial Base (technology transfer to defense suppliers) and Government Facilities (university-based research tied to federal labs).

NEXT STEPS
Partner with two R1 universities and one federal funding agency on a pilot using existing public-domain disclosure violation cases as a validation dataset. Commission a legal review of the scoring model's compliance with Title VI and NSPM-33 implementation guidance. Apply for NSF Research on Research Security (RoRS) funding for the validation study.

samples.title: ChainProof
samples.tagline: Isotopic provenance verification for rare earth materials in defense electronics
samples.summary: A supply chain integrity platform that combines isotopic fingerprinting of rare earth elements with a permissioned distributed ledger to verify country-of-origin claims for defense-critical components, providing DoD prime contractors with auditable provenance from mine to assembly.
samples.brief_text:
CHAINPROOF — Rare Earth Material Provenance for Defense Electronics

CONCEPT
A supply chain integrity platform that uses isotopic geochemical fingerprinting — inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) — to verify country-of-origin claims for rare earth elements (REEs) in defense-critical components. Verified provenance data is recorded to a permissioned distributed ledger shared by DoD prime contractors, sub-tier suppliers, and DCSA. ChainProof provides auditable, tamper-evident REE provenance from extraction site to final assembly.

PROBLEM
The United States sources 90%+ of its rare earth element processing from the People's Republic of China. Neodymium-iron-boron magnets in F-35 actuators, dysprosium in submarine sonar systems, and terbium in night-vision optics all carry undisclosed supply chain exposure to Chinese processors. The 2019 DoD REE supply chain report identified falsified country-of-origin documentation as the primary integrity gap — bad-faith suppliers substitute Chinese-processed REEs while claiming Australian, Canadian, or U.S. origin. DFARS clauses prohibit certain foreign-source REEs but provide no verification mechanism. Contracting officers rely entirely on supplier self-certification.

SOLUTION
ChainProof integrates geochemical science with supply chain infrastructure:
- Isotopic Fingerprinting: REE samples collected at the extraction, processing, and fabrication stages are analyzed via ICP-MS. Isotopic ratios of samarium, neodymium, and cerium are geologically distinct by deposit — Australian Mount Weld bastnäsite has a measurably different isotopic signature from Inner Mongolian bayan obo ore. ChainProof builds and maintains a reference library of certified isotopic fingerprints for production sites.
- Certification Token: each verified REE batch receives a cryptographically signed Provenance Certificate Token (PCT) tied to the ICP-MS result, production site, and chain-of-custody timestamp.
- Permissioned Ledger: PCTs are recorded on a permissioned distributed ledger (Hyperledger Fabric) accessible by DoD prime contractors, validated sub-tier suppliers, and DCSA auditors. The ledger is immutable; substitution after certification is detectable.
- Anomaly Alerts: when a component enters the supply chain with a PCT absent or inconsistent with prior-stage certifications, ChainProof generates an automatic alert to the prime contractor compliance team and DCSA point of contact.
- DFARS Integration: PCT data is formatted for direct ingestion into DFARS compliance reporting workflows.

TARGET USERS
- DoD prime contractors (Lockheed Martin, Raytheon, Northrop Grumman, General Dynamics)
- Sub-tier defense component manufacturers
- DCSA supply chain risk management program
- DLA Strategic Materials
- Congressional oversight (HASC and SASC supply chain subcommittees)

COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE
Blockchain provenance platforms (IBM Food Trust, Everledger) have proven the ledger layer for diamonds and food. No platform has combined geochemical isotopic fingerprinting with a defense-contract-compliant ledger for REEs. DARPA's GAPS program addresses trusted supply chain from a hardware-attestation angle but does not address raw material provenance.

KEY RISKS
- Reference library completeness: isotopic signatures must be established for all plausible alternative sources before the system can detect substitution reliably. Library buildout is a 2–3 year effort.
- Sample integrity: the fingerprinting result is only as reliable as the sample collection protocol. Tamper-evident sampling procedures must be third-party certified.
- Cost-per-lot: ICP-MS analysis runs $200–$800 per sample in commercial labs. Cost model must demonstrate ROI versus DFARS non-compliance liability.
- Classified touchpoints: some REE supply chain intelligence is classified; ChainProof's unclassified ledger will not surface all known bad actors.

CRITICAL INFRASTRUCTURE SECTOR
Defense Industrial Base. Secondary applicability: Critical Manufacturing (motor and generator manufacturing), Energy (REEs in wind turbine generators).

NEXT STEPS
Engage Battelle Memorial Institute or National Renewable Energy Laboratory for ICP-MS reference library co-development. Draft a CRADA proposal with DLA Strategic Materials. Identify one DoD prime contractor pilot partner willing to run a ChainProof verification alongside their existing DFARS certification for a single REE-containing component family. Apply for a DoD SBIR Phase I (topic area: supply chain risk management, materials).

samples.title: APEX
samples.tagline: Psychophysiological readiness index for small-unit commanders
samples.summary: A wearable-plus-AI platform that monitors real-time cognitive load and micro-expression indicators in small-unit leaders during high-stakes simulations, generating a Command Readiness Index to inform rest cycle management and decision sequencing before operational deployments.
samples.brief_text:
APEX — Adaptive Performance Index for Command Readiness

CONCEPT
A wearable biometric and video-analytics platform that monitors psychophysiological indicators of cognitive overload and stress-induced decision degradation in small-unit commanders during training simulations and pre-deployment evaluations. APEX synthesizes heart rate variability (HRV), electroencephalography (in-ear EEG), galvanic skin response, and micro-expression analysis into a Command Readiness Index (CRI) that unit leadership uses to optimize rest cycles, rotation schedules, and cognitive task sequencing.

PROBLEM
Small-unit leadership failures under extreme cognitive load — the point at which accumulated stress, sleep deficit, and decision fatigue converge — are responsible for a disproportionate share of operational errors, friendly fire incidents, and rules of engagement violations in documented case studies. The military has no validated, real-time readiness measure for cognitive and stress states at the small-unit commander level. Fitness-for-duty evaluations are self-reported or observer-rated, both of which fail in high-performance unit culture where admitting degradation carries social cost. Leadership rotation decisions are based on physical fitness metrics, deployment timelines, and commander judgment — not cognitive readiness data.

SOLUTION
APEX integrates physiology and behavioral science:
- Biometric Wearable: a modified in-ear device captures continuous EEG and HRV. A wristband captures galvanic skin response and body temperature. All data is processed on-device; only derived indices are transmitted.
- Micro-Expression Detection: a compact body-camera module captures facial video during After Action Reviews (AARs) and scenario debrief moments. Computer vision identifies Action Unit (AU) combinations from Paul Ekman's Facial Action Coding System (FACS) associated with suppressed stress responses — specifically those invisible to human observers but present in brief involuntary expressions.
- Command Readiness Index: APEX's model synthesizes biometric and micro-expression data into a CRI on a 1–10 scale. CRI is designed to be unit-level visible (the commander's team leader sees the aggregate), not individual-rank visible (the commander's own superior does not see individual scores without the commander's authorization).
- Post-Mission Debrief Integration: APEX time-stamps CRI drops against specific decision points in the training scenario, enabling targeted debrief conversations about the cognitive conditions under which specific decisions were made.
- Rest Cycle Recommendation: APEX generates weekly rest cycle and cognitive task sequencing recommendations for unit leadership based on CRI trend data across the unit.

TARGET USERS
- Special Operations Forces pre-deployment evaluation programs (SOCOM, JSOC)
- Combat Medic / 18D training pipelines (cognitive load under medical decision-making)
- Joint Chiefs CWMD planning teams (decision quality under WMD scenario stress)
- Infantry Officer Course and Advanced Officer Leadership programs
- NATO partner nation small-unit leadership development programs

COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE
Existing military performance monitoring focuses on physical readiness (VO2 max, sleep tracking via Garmin/Whoop). DARPA's Accelerated Learning program and the Army's Synthetic Training Environment address skill acquisition but not real-time readiness monitoring. No validated small-unit command readiness index exists. Academic stress physiology research exists in isolation from operational applications.

KEY RISKS
- Adversarial gaming: if commanders know the measurement model, they can behaviorally suppress indicators. APEX must balance transparency (commanders benefit from seeing their own CRI) against gaming risk.
- Validity in operational context: lab-validated physiological models may not transfer to field conditions with environmental confounders (heat, noise, physical exertion). Field validation studies are the critical path.
- Privacy and command authority: CRI data is sensitive personal health information. Data governance framework must be designed before any pilot, not after.
- Integration with existing AAR culture: APEX's value depends on embedding CRI data into existing debrief practices, not adding a parallel reporting burden.

CRITICAL INFRASTRUCTURE SECTOR / FOLDER
Military Human Performance. Cross-applicability: Emergency Services (first responder incident command cognitive load), Government Facilities (nuclear facility operator decision monitoring).

NEXT STEPS
Partner with USSOCOM's Human Performance program for a proof-of-concept validation study using existing Special Forces A-Team training scenarios. License the FACS AU detection model from a validated academic partner. Engage DARPA Biological Technologies Office (BTO) for an Exploratory Research (ER) submission. Draft IRB protocol for human subjects research in military training environments.

samples.title: WatchPost
samples.tagline: Behavioral analytics for insider threat detection in critical infrastructure control rooms
samples.summary: A software overlay for SCADA-connected control room environments that correlates operator behavioral anomalies, off-hours access patterns, and social engineering precursor signals to generate a tiered insider threat alert — without disrupting operator workflow or exposing surveillance mechanisms to the observed.
samples.brief_text:
WATCHPOST — Insider Threat Early Warning for Critical Infrastructure Control Rooms

CONCEPT
A passive behavioral analytics platform that operates as a software overlay in industrial control room environments, correlating SCADA operator behavioral patterns, enterprise IT anomalies, and physical access log data to detect insider threat precursors and social engineering compromise indicators before a control system incident occurs. WatchPost generates tiered alerts routed to the Security Operations Center without disrupting operator workflow or signaling to the observed individual that monitoring has been triggered.

PROBLEM
Insider threat incidents at critical infrastructure facilities — particularly energy generation, water treatment, and pipeline control facilities — have a documented pre-incident behavioral signature that trained threat assessment professionals can recognize retrospectively but that no current operational technology (OT) security system captures in real time. Existing OT security platforms (Claroty, Dragos, Nozomi) detect technical anomalies in SCADA network traffic. They do not detect behavioral precursors to an insider incident: the operator who begins working unusual shifts, who increases contact with an unvetted external party, who fails to follow normal change-management procedures in ways that individually look like errors but collectively fit a known pre-incident pattern.

SOLUTION
WatchPost is a behavioral analytics layer for the SCADA environment:
- OT Behavioral Baseline: WatchPost establishes individual and population-level behavioral baselines for authorized operators: typical working hours, normal command sequences, typical change authorization patterns, standard communication volumes to external parties.
- Anomaly Correlation Engine: the system continuously correlates:
  - SCADA console behavioral deviations (sequence anomalies, off-hours access, unusual manual overrides)
  - Enterprise IT anomalies from the SIEM layer (unusual email volumes, contact with external parties flagged by threat intel, large file movements)
  - Physical access log data (badge-ins during off-hours, escorted visitors matching an operator's schedule)
- Threat Indicator Taxonomy: WatchPost's detection model is built on CISA's Insider Threat Mitigation Guide indicators and validated ASIS threat assessment behavioral frameworks, adapted for the OT operator context.
- Tiered Alert Routing: alerts route to the facility security officer or regional SOC at three tiers (Monitor / Escalate / Intervene) without generating any visible signal in the control room. Operators are not aware of alert status.
- Case Package Generation: when a human threat assessment professional reviews an alert, WatchPost auto-generates a case evidence package with timestamped behavioral data in the format required for HR, legal, and law enforcement referral.

TARGET USERS
- Energy sector utility operators (generation, transmission, distribution)
- Pipeline operators (natural gas, petroleum)
- Water and wastewater utility security teams
- Nuclear facility security officers (NRC-regulated insider threat programs)
- DHS CISA Critical Infrastructure Security Advisors (CISAs) supporting sector risk management agencies

COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE
Existing insider threat platforms (DTEX, Veriato, Securonix) are designed for enterprise IT environments and track employee computer activity. None have been designed for the OT/SCADA operator context with its distinct behavioral norms, regulatory constraints (NERC CIP, NRC 10 CFR 73), and operational disruption risk. WatchPost is positioned as the first insider threat behavioral detection layer built specifically for the critical infrastructure control room.

KEY RISKS
- NERC CIP compliance: any monitoring system in a BES Cyber System environment must comply with NERC CIP-006, CIP-007, and related reliability standards. Legal review required before deployment in regulated facilities.
- False positive rate: behavioral anomalies in a control room often have benign explanations (shift coverage, training periods, system maintenance). A high false positive rate generates alert fatigue and destroys utility team confidence in the system.
- Labor relations: employee monitoring in unionized facilities may trigger CBA provisions or NLRB considerations. WatchPost's deployment scope must be legally reviewed facility by facility.
- Adversarial adaptation: a sophisticated insider who has conducted threat assessment training may know how to suppress behavioral signals. WatchPost must be periodically validated against red team behavioral evasion scenarios.

CRITICAL INFRASTRUCTURE SECTOR
Energy. Secondary applicability: Water & Wastewater Systems, Nuclear Reactors / Materials / Waste, Transportation Systems (pipeline control), Chemical Sector (chemical plant DCS environments).

NEXT STEPS
Engage a regional electric utility (IOU or municipal) as a pilot partner through an InfraGard sector council introduction. Commission a NERC CIP compliance gap analysis for the WatchPost deployment architecture. Draft the behavioral indicator taxonomy validation protocol with a certified threat assessment professional. Apply for DHS CISA Critical Infrastructure Security and Resilience grant funding (BRIC or SHSP eligible).

samples.title: BioFusion
samples.tagline: 72-hour advance warning of novel biological events through municipal data fusion
samples.summary: A syndromic surveillance platform that fuses hospital ED admission anomalies, OTC pharmacy purchase patterns, environmental sensor data, and emergency dispatch text to provide 72-hour advance warning of novel biological events — before formal public health alert channels are triggered.
samples.brief_text:
BIOFUSION — Syndromic Surveillance Integration at the Municipal Level

CONCEPT
A multi-source data fusion platform that correlates emergency department admission anomalies, over-the-counter pharmacy purchasing patterns, environmental monitoring telemetry, and emergency dispatch text to generate a Municipal Biological Threat Index (MBTI) providing 72-hour advance warning of novel biological events — before the case-count thresholds that trigger formal public health notifications are reached. BioFusion bridges the gap between environmental detection systems (DHS BioWatch) and clinical surveillance systems (CDC BioSense), which currently operate with no real-time integration layer.

PROBLEM
Every documented large-scale biological event — pandemic influenza, anthrax letters, ricin incidents, and naturally occurring outbreak events — has shown the same failure pattern: signal is present in multiple independent data streams days before any single data stream reaches threshold for a formal alert. ED physicians see unusual pneumonia clusters. Pharmacies see unusual antibiotic demand spikes. Environmental sensors flag particulate anomalies. Emergency dispatchers hear unusual complaint patterns. None of these signals are currently fused at the municipal incident command level in real time. The result: the decision advantage that early warning would provide — pre-positioning PPE, activating surge protocols, alerting laboratory capacity — is lost.

SOLUTION
BioFusion is a real-time multi-source fusion platform for municipal health security:
- ED Syndromic Layer: integrates with hospital EHR systems (Epic, Cerner) via HL7/FHIR API to ingest real-time chief complaint and ICD-10 code streams from all emergency departments within the municipal catchment area. Statistical anomaly detection flags unusual cluster emergence.
- Pharmacy Signal Layer: integrates with pharmaceutical distributor point-of-sale data (McKesson, Cardinal Health) for OTC antiemetic, antihistamine, antipyretic, and antidiarrheal purchasing. Unusual geographic or demographic purchase concentration triggers a signal.
- Environmental Layer: ingests DHS BioWatch aerosol monitoring data (where available), EPA air quality monitoring data, and municipal water system SCADA sensor data. Anomalous readings are correlated against clinical signal timing and geography.
- Dispatch Text Layer: NLP processing of emergency dispatch text fields identifies phrase clusters associated with biological exposure presentations ("rash and breathing difficulty," "multiple people vomiting at same location") and aggregates by geography.
- Municipal Biological Threat Index: BioFusion's fusion engine weights and combines signals from all four layers to produce an MBTI on a tiered scale (Green / Yellow / Orange / Red). MBTI feeds directly into the municipal Emergency Operations Center dashboard.
- Advance Warning Output: when MBTI reaches Yellow, BioFusion generates a structured Biological Situational Awareness Report (BSAR) for the public health incident commander that identifies the strongest signal sources, the probable geographic centroid, and the most likely biological agent categories consistent with the current pattern.

TARGET USERS
- Municipal and county public health emergency preparedness offices
- State public health emergency coordinators
- Hospital system emergency management and incident command teams
- DHS CISA (coordination with BioWatch program)
- FBI WMD Coordinators (biological threat assessment)
- HHS ASPR Regional Emergency Coordinators

COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE
CDC BioSense Platform provides ED syndromic surveillance nationally but does not integrate pharmacy, environmental, or dispatch data. DHS BioWatch is an environmental detection network with no clinical or behavioral data integration. Veratect/Metabiota-type platforms monitor global media and travel for outbreak signals but do not integrate municipal data sources. No current platform integrates all four data layers into a municipal-level real-time fusion product.

KEY RISKS
- HIPAA compliance: real-time ED data ingestion requires a Business Associate Agreement with each hospital system and careful de-identification before fusion. Legal architecture must be designed before technical architecture.
- Data latency: real-time pharmacy and ED data feeds vary in update frequency by system. FHIR R4 APIs are not universally available; fallback HL7 v2 integration increases latency and reduces signal quality.
- False alert consequences: a false Red MBTI alert that triggers EOC activation has significant public health and political cost. False positive rate must be validated against historical outbreak data before live deployment.
- Local buy-in: BioFusion requires hospital systems, pharmacies, and the utility to share data they currently do not share. Contractual and governance frameworks take longer than technical development.

CRITICAL INFRASTRUCTURE SECTOR
Healthcare and Public Health / Public Health Security. Cross-sector applicability: Water & Wastewater (biological contamination detection), Chemical Sector (industrial chemical release mimicking biological exposure), Emergency Services (surge protocol activation).

NEXT STEPS
Identify a single mid-sized metropolitan area willing to serve as a pilot through a PHEP cooperative agreement. Engage CDC's Center for Surveillance, Epidemiology, and Laboratory Services for validation dataset access (historical syndromic data). Commission a HIPAA compliance architecture review. Apply for BARDA CBRN broad agency announcement funding for the integration and validation study.

samples.title: ClearPath
samples.tagline: Cross-agency threat assessment case continuity across federal, state, and local jurisdictions
samples.summary: A standardized protocol and supporting platform for threat assessment case handoffs that preserves structured behavioral context across agencies operating at different classification levels, preventing the behavioral narrative from fragmenting when a case crosses jurisdictional boundaries.
samples.brief_text:
CLEARPATH — Cross-Agency Threat Assessment Case Continuity Protocol

CONCEPT
A standardized protocol and secure supporting platform for threat assessment case handoffs between federal agencies, state fusion centers, and local law enforcement. ClearPath preserves the structured behavioral assessment narrative — the chronological record of warning behaviors, intervention attempts, and escalation indicators — when a case crosses jurisdictional boundaries, preventing the behavioral context from dissolving into a file number and a referral memo.

PROBLEM
Threat assessment operates on the principle that concerning behavior occurs in a recognizable, assessable arc: early warning behaviors, escalation indicators, and pre-operational actions. That arc only becomes visible when behavioral data points are connected across time and across the agencies that encountered the individual at different points. The current system breaks the arc at every jurisdictional boundary. A case that begins with a campus threat assessment team, escalates to local law enforcement, gets referred to an FBI Joint Terrorism Task Force, and is later de-escalated back to a fusion center behavioral threat assessment unit has its behavioral record fragmented across four case management systems that do not communicate. The receiving agency reconstructs from scratch, consistently missing context that would inform a more calibrated intervention.

SOLUTION
ClearPath is a case handoff protocol and platform:
- Structured Behavioral Assessment Record (SBAR): a standardized case record format based on validated threat assessment frameworks (WAVR-21 for targeted violence, HCR-20 for recidivism, B-SAFER for domestic violence spillover) adapted for cross-sector use. SBAR captures the behavioral arc chronologically, not as incident snapshots.
- Classification-Aware Tiering: SBAR data is entered at the classification level appropriate to the originating agency. ClearPath generates a Behavioral Context Summary (BCS) — a structured, deidentified distillation of the behavioral arc in an unclassified form — for transfer to receiving agencies without classification authority.
- Chain-of-Custody Documentation: every behavioral assessment entry is time-stamped, author-credentialed, and digitally signed. The chain of custody is maintained continuously, supporting legal admissibility if the case escalates to prosecution.
- Receiving Agency Onboarding: when a case is transferred, the receiving agency threat assessment professional receives the BCS, a structured narrative summary of prior interventions and their outcomes, and the identity of the originating case contact for a direct consult call before closing the intake process.
- Early Warning Behavior Coding: all SBAR entries use a standardized early warning behavior taxonomy (drawn from the NTAC Threat Assessment in Schools framework and the FBI's Making Prevention a Reality study) that enables cross-case pattern recognition when an individual appears in multiple agencies' records.
- Pattern Recognition Dashboard: ClearPath flags when the same individual has records across multiple participating agencies — the cross-institutional connection that the current system cannot see.

TARGET USERS
- FBI Behavioral Analysis Unit and Threat Assessment Unit
- FBI Joint Terrorism Task Force field offices
- State and regional fusion center behavioral threat assessment units
- DHS Office of Intelligence and Analysis Threat Management Division
- Campus and K-12 threat assessment teams (as the upstream intake point in many escalation chains)
- Prosecutors and public defenders in cases where behavioral threat assessment history is relevant

COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE
No current platform provides structured behavioral case handoff across classification tiers. CISA's Threat and Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (THIRA) framework and the National Threat Assessment Center's training programs provide guidance and training but no operational case management system. FBI eGuardian and DHS Suspicious Activity Reporting are tip intake tools, not behavioral assessment or case continuity tools.

KEY RISKS
- Authority to Operate: ClearPath must achieve ATO at CUI and potentially SECRET tiers to be useful for federal participants. This is a multi-year process.
- Privacy Act and civil liberties: maintaining a behavioral record on an individual who has not been charged creates significant legal and civil liberties exposure. Use policy, retention periods, and independent audit mechanisms must be designed before deployment.
- Adoption incentive: federal agencies will not join a platform they do not control or trust. Governance structure (who owns the data, who audits access) is the primary adoption barrier, not technical design.
- Practitioner training: ClearPath's value is proportional to the quality of SBAR entries. Threat assessment practitioners must be trained to the same behavioral taxonomy before the pattern recognition function is reliable.

CRITICAL INFRASTRUCTURE SECTOR / FOLDER
Behavioral Threat Assessment. Cross-sector applicability: Government Facilities (campus and federal workplace threat assessment), Emergency Services (early intervention before law enforcement escalation), Transportation (workplace violence in transportation sector environments).

NEXT STEPS
Convene a working group with FBI BAU, NTAC, one state fusion center, and one university threat assessment team to develop the SBAR taxonomy and validate it against 10 anonymized historical case files. Commission a legal review of the BCS protocol under EO 12333, the Privacy Act, and applicable state public safety information sharing statutes. Identify an appropriate federal case management system sponsor (FBI, DHS, or DOJ) for the ATO sponsorship pathway.
